THE COURT OF APPEAL OF THE DEMOCRATIC SOCIALIST REPUBLIC OF SRI
LANKA
In the matter of an Appeal made under
Section 331(1) of the Code of Criminal
Procedure Act No. 15 of 1979 read with Article
138 of the Constitution of the Democratic
Socialist Republic of Sri Lanka
Hon. Attorney General,
Attorney General's Department,
Colombo
Complainant
Court of Appeal Case No: HCC/86-89/2023
High Court of Hambanthota
Case No: HC 418/2006
1. Aranwela Gamage Upul
2. Jayanthi Wasage Susil Jayabandu
3. Sarambage Siril
4. Pathiraja Mudiyanselage Ranjith
Prasanna Pathiraja
Accused
AND NOW BETWEEN
1. Aranwela Gamage Upul
2. Jayanthi Waasage Susil Jayabandu
3. Sarambage Siril
4. Pathiraja Mudiyansege Ranjith
Prasanna Pathiraja
Accused – Appellants
Page 2 of 26
Vs.
Hon. Attorney General,
Attorney General's Department,
Colombo
Complainant – Respondent
Before: B. Sasi Mahendran, J.
Amal Ranaraja, J
Counsel: Shavindra Fernando PC for the 1st and 4th Accused-Appellant
Anil Silva PC with A Ravindra Silva for the 2nd Accused-Appellant
Dishna Warnakula, DSG for the Respondent
Written 14.05.2024 (by the 1st and 4th Accused-Appellants)
Submission 31.01.2024 (by the 2nd Accused-Appellant)
On: 09.04.2024 (by the 3rd Accused-Appellant)
07.10.2025 (by the Complainant-Respondent)
Judgment On: 10.02.2026
JUDGMENT
B. Sasi Mahendran, J.
The Accused- Appellants (hereinafter referred to as the 1st,2nd,3rd and 4th Appellants)
were indicted before the High Court of Hambanthota on the charge of committing the
offence of murder of Loku Hennadige Amila Mahesh on 29.06.2005, punishable under
Section 296 read with Section 32 of the Penal Code.
Page 3 of 26
At the trial, the prosecution presented evidence through 9 witnesses and marking
productions P1-P4 and thereafter closed its case. The Appellants, in their defence, made
dock statements.
At the conclusion of the trial, the Learned High Court Judge, by judgment dated
15.02.2023, found Appellants guilty of murder and imposed the death sentence.
Being dissatisfied with both the conviction and the sentence imposed by the Learned High
Court Judge, the Appellants preferred an appeal before this Court, articulating the
following grounds in support of their challenge.
1st and 4th Appellants' grounds are as follows,
1. The Learned High Court Judge has mistakenly leveled a charge against the four
Accused under s.32 of the Penal Code for common intention.
2. I. The Learned High Court Judge has failed to consider the INHERENT
INPROBABILITY;
a) If they went to commit the crime with the murderous intention, would they have
officially taken out the weapon making IB entries of such.
b) Going to the house of the deceased, would they have knocked at the door,
identified themselves that they were from the Thissamaharama Police and then
enter the house?
3. The Learned High Court judge has failed to consider whether it is right law to
sustain a conviction on circumstantial evidence;
a) Each item of evidence must be implicating the accused of the crime that they
are charged with.
b) There cannot be any reasonable hypothesis that is consistent with the innocence
of the accused.
4. The Learned High Court Judge has failed to consider the discrepancies in the
evidence of the Prosecution witnesses and has come to a wrong conclusion that the
evidence of the Prosecution witnesses supports each other whereas there were
several differences and discrepancies in the evidence of the Prosecution Witnesses.
Page 4 of 26
5. The Learned High Court Judge has failed to appreciate the entirety of the evidence
led at the trial in the High Court which do not justify the conviction of the
Appellant of the offences set out in the charge of the indictment.
6. The Learned Judge of the High Court Judge has failed to appreciate the items of
evidence in favour of the Appellants which tend to negative their participation in
the incidents which culminate in causing death to the Deceased.
7. The Learned High Court Judge has failed to consider the mitigatory facts and the
circumstances under which the Accused -Appellants have committed the alleged
offence and has thereby erred in law by falling to give due consideration to the code
of Criminal Procedure.
3rd Appellant’s grounds are as follows,
1. Whether the presence of a common intention of the Accused - Appellants had been
properly evaluated?
2. Whether PW 02 and PW 04 are trustworthy and/or reliable witnesses?
3. Whether the identification of the Accused - Appellants was properly constituted at
the trial?
4. Whether the learned High Court Judge has properly evaluated the Dock
Statement of the Appellants?
5. The learned High Court Judge failed to evaluate the facts and evidence of the case
properly when delivering his judgement.
6. The Learned High Court Judge's Judgment fails to meet the requirements of
Section 283(1) of the Criminal Procedure Code
2nd Appellant’s grounds are as follows,
1. Has the Prosecution proved the case against the 2n° accused appellant beyond
reasonable doubt?
2. Has the matters favourable to the accused not been considered by the Learned
Trial Judge and thereby was the 2nd accused appellant deprived of a fair trial.
3. Has the Learned Trial Judge misdirected himself when analyzing the evidence as
to whether the 2nd accused appellant had a common murderous intention?
Page 5 of 26
4. Has the Learned Trial Judge not adequately considered whether in all the
circumstances of this case that there was clear evidence that this incident occurred
as a result of an accident.
5. Has the Learned Trial Judge misdirected himself as regards the tests that should
have been followed in the evaluation of the defence evidence?
The facts and circumstances of this are as follows,
PW 02, Benisha Sasipraba Mendis, the wife of the deceased, testified on 28.06.2005, that
the deceased, her siblings, and her parents were at home. After dinner, taken at
approximately 9:00–9:30 p.m., the deceased and the witness slept in a separate room.
Around 12.00 p.m., the witness’s father knocked on their door, and when the door was
opened, he was accompanied by two unknown Appellants to the witness. Both of them
then took the deceased to the front of the house, and the witness testified that the
appellants appeared to be intoxicated at the time. After the appellants proceeded to
verbally abuse the deceased and the family. The witness, together with her parents,
followed behind the deceased. She observed the events from the light cast on the front
wall and noted that three other individuals were present near the doorway during the
incident.
One appellant who came inside had a gun in his possession, and next, the deceased was
taken to the back side of the house from the right side of the front side by the appellants
who came to take him inside. Then the father of the witness tried to step to the front side,
and the appellants pushed him to the inside of the house. The other three were remaining
near the door. After saying “දුවපන්,” she heard two gunshots from the back of the house.
She further confirmed that the sound did not belong to the deceased. Then both appellants,
who had gone to the back, returned to the front, and together with the others instructed
them to switch off the lights and close the door. Afterwards, they all left the scene. The
witness then fell asleep in the living room. At around 6:00 a.m., the neighbour informed
her about the deceased, and she observed that the body was lying down. The witness also
admitted that she gave a statement to the police at the hospital.
During cross-examination, the witness confirmed that she had given two separate
statements to the Police. The defence highlighted a contradiction, noting that during the
inquest, she had stated that both individuals who entered the house were armed with
guns. It is pertinent to refer to the entire cross‑examination of PW 2, who is the main
witness in this matter.
Page 6 of 26
Page 161 of the brief,
හරස් ප්‍රශ්ණ
ප්‍ර : නමුත් පපොලිසියට කට උත්තර කියක් දුන්නොද ?
උ : එකයි.
ප්‍ර : පදකක් දුන්පන් නැහැ?
උ : ස්පිරිතොපේදී එක කට උත්තරයක් දුන්නො-
ප්‍ර : මොස් පදකකට පස්පපස්ප කට උත්තරයක් ගත්ත ද?
උ : හම්බන්පතොටින් ගත්තො.
ප්‍ර : කට උත්තර පදකක් ගත්තො?
උ : ඔව්.
ප්‍ර : නමුන්පගන් ගරු අධිකරනපයන් විනිශපචකොර තුමන් ඇහුවො ඒ පව්ලොපව්
කවුහරි අපත් තුවක්ුවක් තිබුණ ද කියලො?
උ : ඔව්.
ප්‍ර : තමුන් දැක්පක් එක් පකපනක්පේ අපත් එක තුවක්ුවක් තිපබනවො?
උ : ඔව්.
ප්‍ර : පබනිෂො ශෂිප්‍රභො පමන්ිස්ප තමුන් ස්ොක්ෂි දුත්තො පන්ද මරණ පරීක්ෂණපේදී?
උ : ඔව්.
ප්‍ර : ඒ පව්ලොපව් නමුත් කිව්ව ද පදන්පනක් අපත් තුවක්ු තිබුණො කියලො?
උ : පගදරට ආපු එක්පකපනක් අපත් විතරයි තිබුපන්
ප්‍ර : පදන්පනක් අපත් තිබුණො කියලො කිව්ව ද තමුන් ?
උ : නැහැ.
පදන්පනක් අපත් තුවක්ු තිබුනො යන පකොටස් වී -1 වශපයන් ස්ළුණුපකොට ගරු අධිකරනයට
ඉදිරිපත් කරනවො පම් අවස්පථොපව්දී.
I note that the following facts were not disputed by the defence during cross‑examination:
1. Two unknown people entered the house, one of whom carried a firearm. Later
she got to know that one of them was the 1st Apellant.
2. The 1st Appellant and the other Appellant who was armed with the weapon took
the deceased to the rear of the house.
3. Subsequently, after saying “දුවපන්,” two gunshots were heard.
4. Out of fear, the witnesses did not go outside, and it was the neighbours who later
informed them that the deceased dead body had been found at the back of the
house.
Page 7 of 26
PW 04, Gayani de Silva, the mother‑in‑law of the deceased, corroborating the evidence of
PW 2, held that on the alleged day, the witness and her husband observed a jeep stopping
in front of their house, after which several police officers knocked on the door. The witness
and her husband had opened the door. There were five officers present, and the 1st
Appellant, whom she got to know about from her husband, inquired about the
whereabouts of the deceased. The 1st Appellant, accompanied by another officer, entered
the house while the remaining officers stayed outside. The 1st Appellant was armed with
a gun. Together, they knocked on the door of the room where PW2 and the deceased were
sleeping. When the door was opened, the Appellants took the deceased, stating that there
was something to discuss.
The witness further testified that the deceased was being taken towards the front door,
and they appeared to be intoxicated and used abusive language. The witness testified that
when PW 2 and her husband attempted to step out of the house, the three Appellants
prevented them from doing so. The 1st Appellant, together with another officer, then led
the deceased towards the back of the house. When the deceased was taken to the rear,
those inside heard someone say “දුවිය,” though it was unclear who uttered the words, and
it was not the deceased’s voice. Shortly thereafter, two gunshots were heard. The
Appellants then left the scene. The witnesses did not go to check immediately after
hearing the gunshots, as they were afraid. Early the next morning, neighbours informed
them about an incident that had taken place in Ranawaka Akka’s garden which the house
was located 55 feet away. The witness then saw the body of the deceased, and, together
with PW2 and her husband, went to the scene. When counsel asked whether the deceased
had at any point attempted to escape by jumping out of the window. The witness denied
this suggestion.
It should be noted that the PW 04 admitted to being present in the courtroom while PW
2 was giving her testimony. This circumstance raises concerns about the independence
and reliability of PW 4’s evidence. Consequently, I note that it would not be safe to place
reliance on her testimony. It is pertinent to consider the manner in which the witness was
cross‑examined by the defence.
During cross-examination, the witness acknowledged being present when PW2 testified.
She explained that she awoke after hearing the Appellants knocking and, upon realising
they were police officers, the door was opened. The defense emphasised a contradiction,
noting that in her police statement, she had claimed she only recognised them as police
Page 8 of 26
after the door was opened. The witness clarified that she had also given a statement at
the DIG’s office, bringing the total to three statements. She further explained that the
deceased accompanied the officers willingly, as he had done nothing wrong and made no
attempt to flee. Additionally, she testified that one of the officers who entered the house
was armed.
Page 223 of the brief,
ප්‍ර : කොපේ අපත්ද ආයුධ තිබුපන් ?
උ :පගට ආපු අනිත් පකනොපග අපත් තිබුනො.
ප්‍ර : උපුේ එස්ප.අයි මහත්තයොපග අපත් පනපවයි අනිත් පකනොපග අපත් තමයි තිබුපන් ?
උ : මට මතක හැටියට එපහමයි
In her police statement, she had not mentioned that the 1st Appellant carried a gun,
which the defense marked as an omission. She acknowledged that at the inquest, she
initially stated the 1st Appellant had possession of the gun, but later clarified that she
could only identify the 1st Appellant and that another officer had the weapon. The witness
admitted that the 1st Appellant was known to him, while the others were familiar to him,
though he did not know them by name.
I note that the learned High Court Judge, upon evaluating the evidence of PW 2 and PW
4, held that their consistency, and that any contradictions or omissions, did not affect
their credibility.
Since I am not relying on the evidence of PW4, and noting that the sole eyewitness is PW2,
the issue arises as to whether it is safe to base a conviction solely on the testimony of a
single witness.
I am mindful of how our courts have considered such evidence, recognizing the evidence
of single eyewitness.
Sumanasena v. Attorney General, 1999 (3) SLR 137, Jayasuriya.J held
“In our law of evidence the salutary principle is enunciated that evidence must not
be counted, but weighed and the evidence of a single solitary witness if cogent and
impressive could be acted upon by a Court of law. Section 134 of the Evidence
Ordinance sets out that "no particular number of witnesses shall in any case be
required for the proof of any fact'. In an Indian case the conviction for murder was
affirmed on the mere circumstantial evidence given by a solitary witness and a
Page 9 of 26
pointed reference was made to the principle which we have adumbrated above vide
Mulluwa v. The State of Maddhya Pradesh. Testimony must always be weighed
and not counted and these principles have been followed by Justice G. P. A. De
Silva in Walimunige John v. State; King v. N. A, Fernando(. Thus, the Court could
have acted on the evidence of the solitary witness Nandasena provided the trial
Judge was convinced that he was giving cogent, inspiring and truthful testimony
in Court.”
In Shivaji Sahebrao Bobade v. Maharashtra 1974 (1) SCR 489, Krishna Iyer,J held that;
“In our view there is only one eyewitness, P.W. 5, Vilas. Even if the case against
the accused hangs on the evidence of a single eye-witness it may be enough to
sustain the, conviction given sterling testimony of a competent, honest man,
although as a rule of prudence courts call for corroboration. It is a platitude to say
that witnesses have to be weighed and not counted since quality matters more
than quantity in human affairs. We are persuaded that the PW 5 is a witness for
truth but in view of the circumstances that he is interested, we would still want
corroboration in this case to reassure ourselves. And that we have in this case.”
I am convinced of the evidence of the PW 2 consistent and also there is one contradiction
was marked which doesn’t affect the credibility of the witness.
With regard to the abovce said contradiction I am mindful the openly quoted the judgment
of Bharwada Bhoginbhai Hirjibhai vs State of Gujarat 1983 AIR HC 753;
“… (1) By and large a witness cannot be expected to possess a photographic
memory and to recall the details of an incident. It is not as if a video tape is replayed on
the mental screen. (2) ordinarily it so happens that a witness is overtaken by events. The
witness could not have anticipated the occurrence which so often has an element of
surprise. The mental faculties therefore cannot be expected to be attuned to absorb the
details. (3) The powers of observation differ from person to person. What one may notice,
another may not. An object or movement might emboss its image on one person's mind
whereas it might go unnoticed on the part of another. (4) By and large people cannot
accurately recall a conversation and reproduce the very words used by them or heard by
them. They can only recall the main purport of the conversation. It is unrealistic to expect
a witness to be a human tape recorder. (5) In regard to exact time of an incident, or the
time duration of an occurrence, usually, people make their estimates by guess work on
Page 10 of 26
the spur of the moment 1.1 at the time of interrogation. And one cannot expect people to
make very precise or reliable estimates in such matters. Again, it depends on the time-
sense of individuals which varies from person to person…”
We are also mindful that the learned High Court Judge had the opportunity to observe
the deportment and demeanor of the witness, and has accordingly concluded that the
witness’s testimony is consistent.
There is another point of evidence to consider: PW2 stated that five people were present
and that she heard two gunshots. The defence sought to emphasize the consistency in her
statement. I remain mindful of the observations made in the cited case when assessing
this evidence.
State of Uttar Pradesh v. M.K. Anthony, by D. A. Desai J, reported in Supreme Court
Journal 1984 (2) page 498,
“While appreciating the evidence of a witness, the approach must be
whether the evidence of the witness read as a whole appears to have a ring of truth.
Once that impression is formed, it is undoubtedly necessary for the court to
scrutinize the evidence more particularly keeping in view the deficiencies, draw-
backs and infirmities pointed out in the evidence as a whole and evaluate them to
find out whether it is against the general tenor of the evidence given by the witness
and whether the earlier evaluation of the evidence is shaken as to render it
unworthy of belief. Minor discrepancies on trivial matters not touching the core of
the case, hyper-technical approach by taking sentences torn out of context here or
there from the evidence, attaching importance to some technical error committed
by the investigating officer not going to the root of the matter would not ordinarily
permit rejection of the evidence as a whole. If the court before whom the witness
gives evidence had the opportunity to form the opinion about the general tenor of
evidence given by the witness, the appellate court which had not this benefit will
have to attach due weight to the appreciation of evidence by the trial court and
unless there are reasons weighty and formidable it would not be proper to reject
the evidence on the ground of minor variations or infirmities in the matter of trivial
details. Even honest and truthful witnesses may differ in some details unrelated
Page 11 of 26
to the main incident because power of observation, retention and reproduction
differ with individuals.”
Apart from those contradictions, the witness was consistent on the following matters:
1. The 1st Appellant, along with another, came and asked the deceased to come out
and the other Appellant was armed with a weapon.
2. PW 2 stated that she heard the word “දුවපන්” and two gunshots.
3. PW 2 said that, due to fear, she remained inside with other witnesses.
It should be noted that these facts were not disputed by the defence when she was cross
examined by their counsel. When the facts are not disputed by the defence, they are
treated as proved facts. This dictum was considered in the following judgements.
In Edrick de Silva v. Chandradasa de Silva, 70 NLR page 169 at 170, Justice H.N.G.
Fernando held that,
“Where there is ample opportunity to contradict the evidence of a witness but is
not impugned or assailed in Cross-examination that is a special fact and feature
in the case. It is a matter falling within the definition of the word “Prove” in section
3 of the evidence Ordinance, and a trial Judge or court must necessarily take that
fact into consideration in adjudicating the issue before it.”
I hold that, according to PW2, the appellants took the deceased from his home, led him
behind, and she then heard “දුවපන්” followed by two gunshots.
The main defence advanced by the Appellants was that, on the alleged day, all four of
them went to the deceased’s house with the sole intention of arresting him, as there were
several complaints against him. They contended that their only purpose was to apprehend
the culprit, negating the mens rea element of the murder. This version was corroborated
by the evidence of PW 9, who conducted the internal inquiry. According to the Appellants,
they called out to the residents of the house, revealed their identity, and explained the
purpose of their visit. The 4th Appellant stated that he was positioned behind the house
with a firearm and saw the deceased jumping out of a window. While chasing the deceased,
he fell to the ground after becoming entangled, at which point his firearm discharged
automatically. Upon failing to locate the deceased by searching from the torch, they
assumed he had successfully escaped. They then advised the deceased’s father‑in‑law to
produce him the following morning and returned. He main defence advanced by the
Page 12 of 26
appellants is that they did not take the deceased out of his home, and that the firearm in
the possession of the fourth appellant discharged accidentally.
I am mindful that, at the time the appellants left the residence of the deceased, they were
aware that the firearm in the possession of the fourth appellant had discharged a bullet.
In fact, out of the thirty bullets in that weapon, one was found to be missing. Upon their
return to the police, no entry had initially been made. Only after the murder was reported
did they record an entry and narrate the story, referring to the misfire. I note that this
was considered by the Learned High Court Judge.
Page 400 of the brief,
“පැ.සා.09 වශයෙන් සාක්ෂිෙට කැඳවා ඇත්යත් යෙෙ ෙරණෙ සිදුවීෙ සම්බන්ධයෙන් නිලධාරීන්
සම්බන්ධව විනෙ පරීක්ෂෂණෙ පැවැත්ූ එවකට හම්බන්යතාට යපාලිස් අධිකාරීවරො වශයෙන් යස්වෙ

The reason for the delay was not explained by all appellants in their dock statements.
Thee 1st appellant in his dock statement has stated that they had received information
about the deceased, who was wanted by the police, and together with the other three
Appellants proceeded to the scene. He explained that the 4th Appellant was positioned at
the rear of the house with the firearm, while the 2nd and 3rd appellants were placed near
the door. They knocked on the door, but no one responded. The 1st appellant then
instructed the 2nd appellant, who was known to the residents, to open the door. At that
time, they heard the sound of a gunshot from behind the house. The 4th appellant stated
that when the deceased attempted to run away, he chased him but fell to the ground,
Page 13 of 26
causing his firearm to discharge automatically. The appellants then searched the area but
found nothing. As they returned, the front door was opened and a person appeared. When
questioned about the deceased, that person stated that he had run away and promised to
produce him to the police the following morning. The appellants then left the scene and
returned to the police station.
The 2nd appellant stated that they had gone to arrest the deceased, and that the 1st
appellant knocked on the door but received no response. Since the 2nd appellant was
known to the residents, he inquired about the deceased. At that time, they heard the
sound of a gunshot from the rear of the house. The 2nd appellant, together with the 1st
appellant, ran to the back, where the 4th appellant reported that someone had escaped
through the window. While chasing that person, the 4th appellant fell to the ground. PW
3 later stated that on the following morning he would bring the deceased to the police.
The 3rd appellant stated that, acting on the advice of the OIC, he, together with the other
appellants, went to arrest the deceased. Upon arrival, the 1st appellant positioned the 4th
appellant at the rear of the house and, along with the 2nd appellant, engaged with the
residents. They heard no response from inside the house, but shortly thereafter a sound
was heard from the back. The other three appellants ran towards that direction. When
the 1st appellant inquired about what had happened, the 4th appellant explained that a
person had escaped through the window and, while chasing him, he had fallen to the
ground, causing his firearm to discharge. As they returned to the front, they encountered
a person whom the 2nd appellant identified as “Kalu Mahaththaya.” The 1st appellant
informed him that the deceased was wanted by the police and instructed him to bring the
deceased to the police the following morning.
According to the 4th appellant, went to in the effort to arrest the deceased. The appellants
arrived at the location armed with a firearm. The 1st appellant positioned the 4th
Appellant at the rear of the house with the weapon. Shortly thereafter, the 4th appellant
heard the sound of someone jumping from the left‑side window. While chasing the
individual, he fell to the ground due to the presence of Katuwal plants, at which point his
firearm discharged automatically. The 1st and 3rd appellants then came to the rear of the
house. When questioned by the 1st appellant, the 4th appellant explained the incident
and stated that he had injured his knee. The 1st appellant subsequently searched the
area using a torch. Later, upon calling the owner of the house, identified as Kalu, he
informed the 1st appellant that he had not been aware of their presence, that the deceased
Page 14 of 26
had fled, and that he would produce the deceased at the police station the following
morning. The appellants then returned to the police station and recorded the incident in
the information book. The 4th appellant further stated that he had no knowledge of the
deceased’s death and reiterated that the firearm had discharged automatically when he
fell to the ground.
What is the evidentiary value of such dock statements?
This was considered in CA 186/2009 Kiribandara Muhandiramlage Rohana Tissa Kumara
v. AG Decided on 18.10.2011 W.L.Ranjith Silva, J page 133 (Criminal Law-Unreported
2011 Volume 2)
“A dock statement must be capable of creating a reasonable doubt and not a fanciful
doubt. (CA 89/2001). Even though the Trial Judge failed in his duty to consider the dock
statement adequately and impartially, if no credence can be given to the evidence of
the Appellant then there is no reason for the appellate court interfere with the
conviction. (vide. Dharmadasa v. Director General, Commission to Investigate Briery
and Corruption 2003 1 SLR 64). In Misnagollage Siriyawathie v. The State CA 159/95
(HC Avissawella 72/1992) it was held that even if there is a misdirection by the learned
trial Judge, in view of the overwhelming evidence placed against the accused by the
prosecution, and the correct findings and adjudications reached by the learned trial
judge on all the issues in the case, and in the Light of the cogent and overwhelming
material against the accused there is no reason for the Appellate Court to interfere with
the findings of the learned High Court Judge. It was held by the Court Simongs
Ekanayake v. The Attorney General. CA 129/2005 (HC Anuradhapura 142/2002) that
where the trial judge has the dock, nor miscarriage of justice would take place due to
the lapse of the Trial Judge if there is material to say that the dock statement is palpably
false, then the findings of the trial court should not be overturned.”
In the case of Queen Vs. Kularatne (1968) 71 NLR 529, it was held that while
jurors must be informed that such a statement must be looked upon as evidence
subjected however, to the infirmities that the accused’s statement is not made under
oath and not subjected to cross-examination.
Held further;
1. If the dock statement is believed, it must be acted upon to.
2. If it raised a reasonable doubt in their minds about the case of the prosecution,
Page 15 of 26
the defence must succeed. and;
3. It must not be used against another accused.
The case Sarath v. Attorney General 2006 (3) SLR 96 too had shed light on the issue of
how a dock- statement must be evaluated, wherein, it had been held that;
‘ one must bear in mind that when a dock statement is considered anywhere in the
judgment, the judge who heard the evidence is aware of the prosecution case and would
always consider the dock statement while considering the prosecution story.
One cannot consider the dock statement is isolation. How can one accept or reject
the dock statement without knowing the other side of the story.?”
I note that the learned High Court Judge has rejected the dock statements, having
considered the evidence of PW 7 who is JMO.

"ප්‍ර : ඒ අනුව පදොස්පතර මහත්තයොට පවි උණ්ඩය වැඳීම ස්ම්බන්ධපයන් යම් මතයක් ප්‍රකොශ කරන්න

පම් අනුව පවි තැබූ ස්පථොනයත්, මරණකරුත් අතර දුර ප්‍රමොණය දිගු දුරක් පනොවන අතර පවි තැබූ

අධිකරණ වවදය නිලධොරියොපේ ඉහත කී ස්ොක්ෂි වලට අනුව තුවක්ුව දරණ තැනැත්තො වැටීමකදී
හදිසිපේ ිට වූ උණ්ඩයකින් සිදු වූ තුවොලයක් විය පනොහැකිය. එම තුවොල වල ස්පවභොවය විත්ති වොිකය
කිසිපස්පත් ිළිගත පනොහැකිය.
This opinion of the JMO directly cut across the fourth appellant’s version that the firearm
misfired accidentally. I am also mindful that the defence did not propose to challenge or
contradict the evidence presented by the JMO. Further, I observe that the body was found
in the neighbourhood. The 1st appellant stated that, after being informed by the 4th
appellant about the misfire, he went in search of the body with a torch but claimed he
could not locate it. There remains a doubt as to whether he in fact undertook such a search.
Considering the evidence of PW 2 together with the testimony of the JMO that the bullet
had travelled in a horizontal line from close range, and noting also the failure to record
entries at the relevant time, the learned High Court Judge has correctly concluded that
the defence evidence did not cast any doubt upon the prosecution’s case.
I have already indicated that the Learned High Court Judge has duly considered the
evidence of PW 2. I find no reason to interfere with the said decision of the Learned High
Court Judge.
Page 17 of 26
Now I consider the evidence of PW 5.
In the case of Pantis Vs. The Attorney General (1998) 2 SLR 148, Wijeraratne J., held
that;
“As the burden of proof is always on the prosecution to prove its case beyond
reasonable doubt and no such duty is cast on the accused and it is sufficient for the
accused to give an explanation which satisfies the Court or at least is sufficient to
create a reasonable doubt as to his guilt.”
In the case of Narender Kumar Vs. State of Delhi (NCT of Delhi, AIR 2012 SC 2281), it
was held: (this case was referred by Justice Sampath B. Abayakoon, J. in Latheef Ihusan
and another v. AG, CA/HCC/0088/2022, 29-04-2024)
“Prosecution case has to stand on its own legs and cannot take support from the
weakness of the case of the defence. However great the suspicion against the
accused and however strong the morel belief and conviction of the Court, unless
the offence of the accused is established beyond reasonable doubt on the basis of
legal evidence and material on the record, he cannot be convicted for the offence.
There is an initial presumption of innocence of the accused and the prosecution
has to bring home the offence against the accused by reliable evidence. The accused
is entitled to the benefit of every reasonable doubt.”
According to PW 05, IP Brabath Deshabandu, who worked in the same police station as
the OIC with the Appellants stated that the neighbor of the deceased, Shriyani Mallika,
informed the police that on 29.06.2005 at around 6:40 a.m., there was a dead body in the
garden. Thereafter, the witness, accompanied by seven officers, proceeded to the scene.
He described the area as open, with some Maduwal plants. The body was lying face
downwards, bearing a gunshot wound to the neck, and blood was scattered in the
surrounding area. The witness stated that PW 2 identified the deceased and stated that
the police had previously knocked on the door asking for him, at which time the deceased
had jumped out of the window and fled. The witness further noted that the window had
no grills, and on the other side there were pieces of wood scattered. He opined that the
scattering of the wood might have occurred when someone stepped on it.
According to the information gathered from the appellants, the witness learned that the
1st appellant had positioned the 4th appellant at the rear of the house in case the deceased
attempted to escape. Based on the appellants’ statements, the witness further understood
Page 18 of 26
what transpired when the deceased was running away. It was revealed that the
appellants had introduced themselves as officers of the Thissamaharama Police and
instructed the deceased to come out, stating that he was wanted by the police in
connection with several cases. They also informed him about a gunshot. At the time, the
appellants were in possession of a T56 firearm and 30 bullets.
The witness stated that he became aware of the actions of the appellants and, from his
observations, noted the location of the deceased’s body, the place where the 4th appellant
had fallen, and the direction in which the deceased had fled. He testified that when the
4th appellant chased the deceased after he had jumped from the window, the appellant
called out for him to stop. However, the 4th appellant fell to the ground due to the presence
of Maduwal plants. The witness explained that he learned these details from the 1st and
4th appellants. He further confirmed that he was able to identify all four appellants.
According to their account, when the 4th appellant fell, the firearm he had prepared was
automatically discharged. The weapon was taken into custody by the witness from the
4th appellant. Subsequently, a riotous situation arose in the area. The witness further
stated that the appellants surrendered the T56 rifle, which at that time contained only 29
bullets. He also testified that both the ASP and the SSP conducted an inquiry regarding
this matter.
During cross‑examination, the witness asserted that there had been many complaints
against the deceased. The witness stated that PW 2 had mentioned the deceased
attempted to escape through the window, and that PW 4 had confirmed this statement.
From the evidence, PW 2 stated that the deceased escaped through the window. This
stands in contrast to the testimony of PW 2, who elsewhere maintained that the deceased
was taken away by the Appellants.
It should be noted that the evidence of PW 2, who stated that the deceased escaped
through the window, was duly considered by the learned High Court Judge. I am mindful
that when PW 2 gave her testimony, her evidence was not contradicted. Her evidence has
not been impeached by the defence under Section 145 (1) or 155 of the Evidence Ordinance.
Generally, the defence seeks to highlight contradictions between earlier statements and
the testimony given in court. This concept was considered in The Law of Evidence, Volume
II, Book 2, E.R.S.R. Comaraswamy, at page 754:
Page 19 of 26
(iv) Section 145 and Section 155 ( c ) should be read together
section 145 and Section 155 ( c ) deal with different aspects of the same matter and
should be read together. The following points of distinction may be noted:
(a) Section 155 ( c ) permits the credit of a witness to be impeached by proof of
former statements that are inconsistent with any part of his evidence, which
may be contradicted. But it lays down no procedure for purpose. That procedure
is laid in Section 145.
(b) Section 155 ( c ), therefore, presupposes the existence and binding effect od
Section 145, and is in a sense controlled by the provisions of Section 145.
According to PW 5, PW 2 had stated that the deceased ran away through the window
when they arrived early in the morning at about 6.40 a.m. In contrast, PW 2 stated that
she gave evidence in the evening at the DIG’s office, which was duly recorded. The
question that arises is whether the first statement, in which PW 2 said that the deceased
ran away, was in fact recorded. According to Section 110 (1) of the Criminal Procedure
Code, when a police officer conducts an investigation, any statement made by a person
disclosing relevant facts must be in writing. It is pertinent to refer to section 110(1) of the
Criminal Procedure Code,
“110.(1) Any police officer or inquirer making an investigation under this Chapter
may examine orally any person supposed to be acquainted with the facts and
circumstances of the case, and shall reduce into writing any statement made by
the person so examined, but any oath or affirmation shall not be administered to
any such person. The whole of such statement shall be recorded in full in the
manner set out in section 109 (2). If the police officer or inquirer asks any question
in clarification such question and the answer given thereto shall be recorded in
form of question and the answer. Such record shall be shown or read to such person
or if he does not understand the language in which it is written, it shall be
interpreted to him in a language he understands and he shall be at liberty to
explain or add to his statement. The person making the statement shall then sign
that statement immediately at the place where the statement inconcluded. The
police officer or inquirer recording the statement shall append below each
statement recorded by him the following certificate:-
"I……hereby declare that I have faithfully and accurately recorded the statement
of the above named…………….”
Page 20 of 26
In fact, this witness stated that he had taken the relevant books known as අපරොධ පරීක්ෂණ
පපොත්. When I analyse the evidence recorded at the trial, much of what he revealed
indicates that he had seen the appellants’ statements, which were recorded at a later
stage. Although we do not have the right to peruse the information book, it can be
concluded from his evidence that he did not record the statements at the time of the
investigation. If he has recodeded the particular statement would have been produced to
the courts under section 110(3) of the Criminal Code,
(3) A statement made by any person to a police officer in the course of any
investigation may be used in accordance with the provisions of the Evidence
Ordinance except for the purpose of corroborating the testimony of such person in
court:
Provided that a statement made by an accused person in the course of any
investigation shall only be used to prove that he made a different statement at a
different time.
Anything in this subsection shall not be deemed to apply to any statement falling
within the provisions of section 27 of the Evidence Ordinance or to prevent any
statement made by a person in the course of any investigation being used as
evidence in a charge under section 180 of the Penal Code.”
The reason for my conclusion is that, during PW 2's evidence, the defence has not
suggested the deceased had run away through the window. Even to the PW 4 this was not
suggested. On the other hand, PW 5 simply said that PW 2 told to him that the deceased
had run away from the window. As I mentioned earlier since they were all working
together in the same police station, I hold that PW 5 is an interested witness,
notwithstanding that he was summoned by the prosecution. I note that his testimony has
not shaken the evidence of PW 2 and PW 4, as defence could not impeach them.
The question before us is whether all four accused shared a common intention to murder
the deceased. As there are no eyewitnesses to identify who actually fired off the fatal shot,
I note that the case rests entirely upon circumstantial evidence. It is incumbent upon the
prosecution to establish, beyond a reasonable doubt, that the Appellants shared the
common murderous intention to commit the offence.
I note that all 4 Appellants were charged for murder under Section 32 of the Penal Code.
Section 32 reads as follows,
Page 21 of 26
32. When a Criminal act is done by several persons in furtherance of
the common intention of all, each of such persons is liable for that
act in the same manner as if it were done by him alone.
The prosecution must establish that the Appellants were actively involved by proving
their participation, presence, prior arrangement, and murderous intent in order to
demonstrate a shared common intention. The following cases give guidance on how
Section 32 should be applied. The applicable principles for common intention are clearly
laid down
in Raju and Others v Attorney General (2003) 3 Sri L.R. 116 at page 119, where
Edirisuriya J held that,
"it seems to me that the learned trial judge has failed to refer to the required
mental element i.e. the murderous intention to constitute the offence of murder
In the case of King v Assappu 50 N.L.R. 324 Dias, J. sitting with Nagalingam, J.
and Gratiaen, J. held that in a case where the question of common intention arises
the Jury must be directed that –
i. The case of each accused must be considered separately.
ii. The accused must have been actuated by a common intention with the doer of
the act at the time the offence was committed.
iii. Common intention must not be confused with same or similar intention
entertained independently of each other.
iv. There must be evidence either direct or circumstantial of pre-arrangement or
some other evidence of common intention.
v. The mere fact of the presence of the accused at the time of the offence is not
necessarily evidence of common intention.
Justice Sirimanne in the case of Punchi Banda v The Queen 74 N.L.R.494 refers
to the legal principle laid down in King v Assappu (supra) that a common
murderous intention must be shared before a person can be convicted of murder
on an application of section 32 of the Penal Code”
Page 22 of 26
In the case of Somarathne v. The Attorney General, 1986 (1) SLR 217 at page 221,
Moonemalle, J held that;
‘it is necessary for the prosecution to prove two essential ingredients, namely, a
sharing of a common intention by the accused and participation of the accused in
the commission of those offences. It was necessary for the trial Judge to apply the
rule of common intention to the facts of the case. Where the evidence before the
trial Judge was circumstantial, then it was his duty to pay heed to the principle
that the inference of common intention should not be reached unless it is a
necessary inference, an only inference, an inference from which there is no escape.
The learned trial Judge has been silent on all these important factors relating to
the rule of common intention. He should have considered them along with the
evidence in the case which he should have carefully analysed before coming to any
finding on the question whether the 2nd accused acted in furtherance of a common
intention with the other accused to commit the offences of house breaking and theft’
This judgement is considered in Athige Don Preeman Melat Silva v The Attorney
General CA/HCC/ 192/2018, decided on 20.06.2022.
It is tried law that common intention can be drawn from the evidence if it could be shown
that if it was the only possible irresistible inference that could have form in the instant
case. It is established that the Appellants had made an outgoing entry with the intention
of arresting the deceased. The 4th appellant admitted that he had accidentally discharged
his firearm. Also, I note that all 4 Appellants denied having arrested the deceased. There
is no evidence to suggest that they had any pre-plan to commit murder. Consequently, the
prosecution bears the burden of proving that all appellants shared a common intention to
kill the deceased through their actions.
According to the above judgements, the common murderous intention must be shared by
all accused for conviction of a murder on an application of Section 32 of the Penal Code.
What is the circumstantial evidence placed against the Appellants?
1. By their own entries in the Information Book and dock statements, the appellants
admitted all four went to the deceased’s residence
2. PW 4 testified she identified the 1st Appellant
3. PW 2 stated that the 1st Appellant, with another who had a gun, took the
deceased out of the house (PW 4 also admitted the fact that he had a gun)
Page 23 of 26
4. The utterance “දුවපන්” was not contradicted by the Appellants.
5. The other two Appellants prevented witnesses from leaving the house, and they
were verbally abusing them.
6. After “දුවපන්,” PW 2 heard two gunshots.
7. Preventing witnesses from leaving the house shows participation of the other two
appellants in the offence.
8. The 4th appellant possessed the firearm, and his claim of no intention was
contradicted by JMO’s evidence (fatal injury at close range).
9. 1st appellant claimed he searched for the body, but it was found near the house.
10. All four appellants knew the gun was fired, but concealed it until a complaint
was made.
11. All four left the scene together and failed to report the matter to superiors.
12. Several hours later, they made an entry claiming the incident was an accident
and there is no explaination by them as the reason for the delay.
Is it not sufficient to prove beyond reasonable doubt that they shared the common
intention? It should be noted that, under Section 32 of the Penal Code, a person can only
be convicted of murder if a common murderous intention is shared among the participants.
His Lordship Justice Kodagoda observed that if there is no evidence of any prior
arrangement, still common intention can be proved by the proving the conduct of
Appellant at the time of the incident and the subsequent conduct. From this evidence the
prosecution can establish that they all shared the common murderous intention to commit
that offence.
This was considered in Sri Lanka Henayalage Somadasa v. Attorney General, SC Appeal
No: 130/2014, Decided on 25.11.2024, Yasantha Kodagoda, PC, J held that;
“Conduct of the Appellant at the time of the incident – Chathurangani being the
sole witness with regard to the commission of the offence has testified that at the
time of the attack on Neelawathi and Nishadini, the 2nd Accused (Appellant)
merely stood near the Andara Weta (a reference to the hedge at the perimeter of
the compound). As pointed out by learned counsel for the Appellant, given the
possible distance between the location of the house and the place where the
Appellant was standing, it cannot even be reasonably concluded that the Appellant
was immediately ‘present’ at the scene of the crime. Furthermore, there is no
evidence of the 2nd Accused being armed or having made any violent utterance. In
Page 24 of 26
my view, this evidence coupled with the previous conduct of the 2nd Accused in no
way justifies a reasonable inference that the Appellant entertained a common
murderous intention along with the 1st and 3rd Accused or that the Appellant’s
own conduct was actuated by a common intention between the three Accused.
Appellant’s subsequent conduct - I must advert to and comment upon another key
submission made by learned Senior ASG. His argument was that the Appellant
having fled away from Delana (possibly along with the 1st and 3rd Accused) on the
night of the incident, not resided at his house for quite some time, absconded and
having subsequently surrendered, supports the inference that he (the Appellant)
shared a common murderous intention together with the other two Accused.
First, one needs to consider the relevancy of these items of evidence. It is common
and quite popular in our criminal justice system to treat such evidence as
‘subsequent conduct influenced by the fact in issue’ and therefore deemed ‘relevant’
under section 8(2) of the Evidence Ordinance. At first sight, it is quite so. However,
given the other evidence of this case, one needs to carefully consider whether such
subsequent conduct was in fact ‘influenced by the facts in issue’ (the complicity of
the Appellant in the commission of the crime) or was influenced by the fact that
other peasants of this township of Delana had assumed that all three Accused had
participated in the dastardly murder of Neelawathi, and therefore that night
caused mischief by setting fire to the houses of all three Accused. Given a degree
of vigilantism which at times prevail in our society in instances where there is
considerable public disquiet as a reaction towards the commission of serious crimes,
where segments of the community acting illegally assume the role of the police
(law enforcement) and the courts (judiciary), and apprehend and summarily
punish alleged perpetrators and proceed even to the extent of causing mischief to
their property, had the Appellant remained in the village of Delana, in all
probability he would have been harmed alongside damage being caused to his
house. Therefore, as explained in his testimony, the Appellant having initially
returned to Delana from Kuliyapitiya and thereafter fled the area, lay in hiding
and later surrendered, in my view is quite understandable. Such conduct on the
part of the Appellant cannot in my view be necessarily attributed to his complicity
in the crimes committed by the 1st and 3rd Accused.
However, I must hasten to record that, I do not find anything contrary to law in
the learned trial judge having permitted such evidence relating to ‘subsequent
Page 25 of 26
conduct’ being led by the prosecution. The error arose when such evidence was
analysed, weighed and the learned Judge arrived at the conclusion that the
Appellant entertained a common intention together with the 1st and 3rd Accused
in committing the murder of Neelawathi.
It is necessary to recall that in The King vs. Jan Singho [41 NLR 573], Justice
Moseley sitting in the Court of Criminal Appeal has expressed the view that, the
Appellants (a) having been seen in company with the principal perpetrator at the
time of or immediately after the incident, (b) that they were seen running away
from the scene of the incident, and (c) that they subsequently were not to be found
at their respective homes, were insufficient to arrive at an irresistible inference
that a common intention existed between the Appellants and the principal
perpetrator. In fact, Justice Moseley has proceeded to express the view that (with
which I find myself in respectful agreement) if the Appellant’s actions are capable
of innocent explanation, even though such explanation is not given, the benefit of
such explanation should accrue to the Appellant. In contrast, in this case, the
Appellant has in fact offered the explanation for his subsequent conduct.”
In the instant case, there is evidence to show that when the 1st and the other appellant
took the deceased and while the others stopped the witnesses from intervening.
Thereafter, all went together, and following the incident they failed to make notes, later
claiming it was an accident a position rejected by PW 7. I am also mindful that their
subsequent conduct in not entering notes detracts from any claim of innocence. I am
further mindful that the learned High Court Judge has correctly concluded the evidence
of the prosecution. Although he did not specifically refer to the evidence of PW 5, this
omission does not create any doubt in the prosecution’s version that the appellants took
the deceased and shot him.
I am mindful that the learned High Court Judge correctly concluded that the Appellants
version did not create any doubt in the prosecution’s case. The burden of proof that the
appellants murdered Loku Hennadige Amila Mahesh was discharged by the prosecution
beyond reasonable doubt. The learned High Court Judge analysed the evidence
comprehensively and without compartmentalisation.
In those circumstances, I am not inclined to interfere with the judgment delivered by the
learned High Court Judge dated 15.02.2023 together with the sentencing order.
Page 26 of 26
Accordingly, the appeal is dismissed.
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
Amal Ranaraja, J.
I AGREE
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
